coredata.dc:title: Room temperature phosphorescence based on nitrogen-phosphorus co-doped carbonized polymer dots for information encryption
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Chuan
Zhang, Qinbin
Zhang, Yichuan
Guo, Mingming
coredata.dc:description: Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) are proposed as a novel room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials that widely used in optoelectronic devices, bioimaging, especially in the field of information encryption and anti-counterfeiting due to their unique optical properties. However, achieving RTP emission under matrix-free conditions requires reasonable structural design to prevent the quenching of triplet excitons, which is very challenging. Herein, we proposed a facile, rapid and effective strategy to prepare matrix-free nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped CPDs (named Arg-CPDs) via microwave assisted heating treatment of arginine (Arg) and phosphoric acid. The Arg-CPDs exhibit bright blue fluorescence in aqueous solution and yellow green RTP emission in solid state, and the RTP emission is visible to the naked eye for about 4 s after switching-off the UV lamp. The phosphorescence lifetime of Arg-CPDs was 149 ms and 667 ms in ambient conditions and low temperature (77 K), respectively, which shown that the typical phosphorescent properties. Moreover, potential applications of the Arg-CPDs in the fields of information protection and encryption are demonstrated. This study provides a facile strategy for the preparation of matrix-free CPDs-based RTP materials toward the information security and their successful application in information security.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-phosphorus co-doped carbonized polymer dots
Room temperature phosphorescent
Fluorescence
Information encryption

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities : Southwest University ( 5230200472 ). The authors would like to thank Tianyang Hu from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the phosphorescence analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM analysis of Arg-CPDs; (b) HRTEM analysis of Arg-CPDs; (c) Particle size distribution analysis of Arg-CPDs; (d)XRD analysis of Arg-CPDs.

(a) FTIR spectra of Arg-CPDs; (b) Full XPS spectra of Arg-CPDs (inset: element and atomic percentage); (c-f) High-resolution XPS results for C 1 s (c), N 1 s (d), O 1 s (e), and P 2p (f) spectra of Arg-CPDs.

(a) UV-Vis absorption spectrum and FL emission and excitation spectra of the Arg-CPDs (inset: photograph of aqueous solution under daylight and UV light); (b) Excitation-dependent FL spectra of Arg-CPDs aqueous solution; (c) Fluorescence decay curve of Arg-CPDs aqueous solution, recorded at emission wavelengths of 445 nm under excitation at 365 nm.

(a) Photographs of Arg-CPDs powder under daylight, UV lamp (365 nm) On and Off; (b) FL and RTP spectra of the Arg-CPDs powder under excitation at 365 nm; (c) UV-Vis absorption and phosphorescence emission and excitation spectra of the Arg-CPDs powder. (d) RTP decay spectra and fitting curve of the Arg-CPDs powder at ambient conditions; (e) Schematic illustration of the FL and RTP mechanism for the Arg-CPDs.

Photographs of the Arg-CPDs-based ink and potential applications. (a) Information encryption; (b) Images of the badge of Southwest University on a commercial filter paper with turn-on and turn-oﬀ UV irradiation at 365 nm.

head.ce:title: Room temperature phosphorescence based on nitrogen-phosphorus co-doped carbonized polymer dots for information encryption

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) are proposed as a novel room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials that widely used in optoelectronic devices, bioimaging, especially in the field of information encryption and anti-counterfeiting due to their unique optical properties. However, achieving RTP emission under matrix-free conditions requires reasonable structural design to prevent the quenching of triplet excitons, which is very challenging. Herein, we proposed a facile, rapid and effective strategy to prepare matrix-free nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped CPDs (named Arg-CPDs) via microwave assisted heating treatment of arginine (Arg) and phosphoric acid. The Arg-CPDs exhibit bright blue fluorescence in aqueous solution and yellow green RTP emission in solid state, and the RTP emission is visible to the naked eye for about 4 s after switching-off the UV lamp. The phosphorescence lifetime of Arg-CPDs was 149 ms and 667 ms in ambient conditions and low temperature (77 K), respectively, which shown that the typical phosphorescent properties. Moreover, potential applications of the Arg-CPDs in the fields of information protection and encryption are demonstrated. This study provides a facile strategy for the preparation of matrix-free CPDs-based RTP materials toward the information security and their successful application in information security.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-phosphorus co-doped carbonized polymer dots (Arg-CPDs) with significant room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) emission were synthesized by microwave assisted heating in only about 90 s.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Information security has become an increasingly important issue in our daily communication, trade secrets and national defense, etc., with the development of related industries. Developing some emerging information encryption and anti-counterfeiting materials is an important way to solve this problem [1–3]. Long-life room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials can still emit light for several seconds after the external excitation light source is turned off, which is not available for fluorescent materials and it is usually more difficult to be imitated and counterfeited [4,5]. At present, RTP materials are widely used in information encryption [6,7], anti-counterfeiting [8,9] and optoelectronic devices [10,11] due to their unique optical response performance. However, the reported metal doped inorganic RTP materials or organic metal RTP materials confront the problems of high physiological toxicity, poor stability, high cost and complex preparation process, which greatly limit the further application of these materials [12,13]. Therefore, it is urgent to develop some new RTP materials to meet these challenges.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a kind of light-emitting zero dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, which can be divided into carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nano dots (CNDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [14]. Compared with traditional metal-based quantum dots, they present the advantages of excellent optical stability, biocompatibility, rich and extensive raw materials and simple preparation methods [15,16]. Based on its excellent properties, it is widely used in catalysis [17], sensing [18], printing ink [19], biological imaging [20,21], electronic devices [22] and other fields. Since Zhao et al. [23] first discovered RTP emission of CDs embedded in polyvinyl alcohol matrix in 2013, researchers are no longer confined to fluorescence (FL) emission of CD photoluminescence (PL), and are committed to exploring RTP emission of CDs. In short, there are two key factors to build efficient RTP materials. One is to promote the electron intersystem crossing (ISC) from singlet to triplet, and the other is to reduce the non-radiative transition efficiency of triplet excitons [24]. However, low radiative transition efficiency、inefficient ISC process and being easily quenched by oxygen and water are the biggest difficulties faced by CDs in realizing RTP emission. The results shown that doping into the matrix can not only effectively inhibit the non-radiative transition process caused by the molecular vibration of the light-emitting group, but also effectively isolate the quenching process of the triplet exciton caused by oxygen and water [25]. Therefore, researchers doped CDs into various matrices, such as polyvinyl alcohol [26], cyanuric acid [27], polyurethane [28], zeolite [29], inorganic salt [30] and boric acid [31], thus successfully realizing RTP emission of CDs. For example, Zhou et al. [25] compounded the CDs with urea, boron oxide, polyvinyl alcohol and aluminum sulfate to prepare a series of long-life phosphorescent CDs based composites.

However, most of the reported CDs based RTP materials are materials in which CDs are embedded in the matrix to achieve RTP emission. But the introduction of matrix not only increases the complexity of synthetic materials, but also changes the solubility and physiological toxicity or other inherent properties of CDs [32,33]. The important reason why it is difficult to realize the RTP emission of matrix-free CDs is that the light-emitting groups on the surface of CDs are very vulnerable to the environment without the protection of the matrix, which leads to the quenching of triplet excitons. In recent years, researchers have been trying to develop matrix-free CDs with RTP emission [34,35]. Nie et al. [36] synthesized matrix-free nitrogen doped RTP emitting CDs by hydrothermal method using urea, tartaric acid and acrylamide as reaction precursors, and used them as probes for temperature monitoring and advanced ink for information encryption. These works have proved the feasibility of RTP emission of matrix-free CDs. However, reports on the RTP emission behavior of matrix-free CDs are still very rare, because the triplet excitons without matrix protection is easily affected by the environment and cannot survive for a long time to emit phosphorescence.

In this work, we report a simple and rapid one-step synthesis strategy to prepare nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) co-doped CPDs (Arg-CPDs), which shows significant RTP emission without matrix. Specifically, Arg-CPDs are synthesized by microwave assisted heating using arginine (Arg) and phosphoric acid as precursors. After the reaction of phosphoric acid with Arg containing rich functional groups, the synthesized Arg-CPDs have rich surface states. Doped N and P elements can effectively promote spin orbit coupling (SOC) and the production of triplet excitons. In addition, the intertwined polymer chain structure on the surface of Arg-CPDs not only stabilizes the triplet exciton to reduce the non-radiative transition rate, but also effectively insulates the quenching of the triplet exciton by oxygen and water. Therefore, the matrix-free Arg-CPDs exhibit RTP emission behavior in the ambient conditions. The RTP life of Arg-CPDs powder is 149 ms, and the yellow green afterglow can still be observed for several seconds with the naked eye after switching-off the 365 nm UV lamp, which shows great application potential in information encryption, anti-counterfeiting and other fields.

The L-Arginine (Arg) (molecular weight 174.20, purity 99.0%) and L-Lysine (Lys) (molecular weight 146.20, purity 99.0%) used in the experiment were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Phosphoric acid was purchased from ChuanDong Chemical Reagent Company (Chongqing, China). The experimental water was ultra-pure deionized water (18.25 MΩ cm−1). All chemicals were analytical reagent grade and used as received without any further purification.

The morphology and size of the Arg-CPDs and Lys-CPDs were studied using field emission transmission electron microscope (TEM and HRTEM) (Talos F200X, FEI) with an accelerating voltage as 200 KV. The height dimensions of Arg-CPDs were studied using an atomic force microscope (AFM) of model Bruker Dimension ICON. The analysis of Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) were collected by using TGA 4000-Frontier FTIR (Perkin Elmer). Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were collected by 500 MHz NMR spectroscopy (Agilent, USA). Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was studied using a TA Q20 differential scanning calorimeter with a scan rate of 10 °C min−1. XRD patterns were obtained on an X-ray diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (Rigaku Ultma IV). The XPS spectra of powder samples were recorded by an X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha). Steady-state fluorescence spectra were obtained on a Duetta spectrophotometer (Horiba). Fluorescence lifetime, phosphorescence lifetime, phosphorescence spectrum and afterglow decay curve were measured on an Edinburgh FLS-1000.

A typical synthetic route is described as follow. First, 1.0 g Arg was dissolved in 10 mL deionized water, and then 1.2 mL phosphoric acid was added into the Arg aqueous solution drop by drop with stirring. Then, the transparent solution was heated in a 700 W household microwave oven for 90 s, and the product was a brownish yellow colloidal solid. After the product was cooled to room temperature, 20 mL deionized water was added to fully dissolve it, and then the crude product was centrifuged at the rate of 10000 r/min for 10 min and filtered through 0.22 µm membrane filter to remove large or agglomerated particles. The supernatant was collected and subjected to dialysis (MWCO:1000 Da) for 24 h to remove unreacted precursors and other small molecular impurities. Finally, the Arg-CPDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying. The synthesis of lysine-based CPDs (named Lys-CPDs) without RTP emission is exactly the same as that of Arg-CPDs, except that L-arginine is replaced by L-lysine.

Typical formulation: 40 mg Arg-CPDs and 40 mg Lys-CPDs powder were added to 10 mL ultrapure water for preparing the Arg-CPDs-based ink and Lys-CPDs-based ink, respectively. They can be directly used as encryption inks for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting. The two types of inks were directly inhaled by commercial pens and used to write numbers and patterns.

To prove the successful preparation of Arg-CPDs, the morphology and structure of Arg-CPDs were preliminarily analyzed. TEM ( Fig. 1a) shows that Arg-CPDs have good dispersion. The size distribution (Fig. 1c) shows that the size range of Arg-CPDs is 8.6–15.9 nm and the average size is about 11.6 nm. HRTEM images (Fig. 1b) shows that Arg-CPDs have no obvious lattice, indicating that their carbon cores are composed of amorphous carbon. The AFM image (Fig. S1, Supporting Information) shows that Arg-CPDs are monodisperse, with height data in the range of 7–12 nm. According to the lateral size and height size by TEM and AFM, it can be inferred that the shape characteristics of Arg-CPDs are quasi spherical, which proves the successful preparation of Arg-CPDs nanoparticles. In addition, the XRD spectrum (Fig. 1d) shows that Arg-CPDs produce a wide diffraction peak at about 15–35°, which is a typical amorphous carbon peak [37]. This result is consistent with the HRTEM results. To prove the successful synthesis of Lys-CPDs, TEM was also performed on Lys-CPDs. As shown in Fig. S2a (Supporting Information), TEM shows good dispersion of Lys-CPDs, with an average particle size of 9.6 nm. HRTEM image (Fig. S2b, Supporting Information) shows that Lys-CPDs have an interplanar spacing of 0.19 nm, which corresponds with the basal spacing of graphite [38].

To further analyze the structures of Arg-CPDs, the chemical composition and functional groups of Arg-CPDs are measured by FT-IR, XPS and NMR. As seen in Fig. 2a, the broad and pronounced absorption peaks at 3600 cm−1 to 3100 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of O-H and N-H bonds, respectively. The wide absorption of 3050 cm−1 to 2600 cm−1 and the absorption at 1455 cm−1 represented the stretching vibration and bending vibration of C-H, respectively. The stretching vibration of CO at 1652 cm−1, the bending vibration of N-H at 1552 cm−1 and the vibration of C-N at 1255 cm−1 indicate the existence of amide bond in Arg-CPDs. It is worth noting that the characteristic peaks at 1225 cm−1, 1068 cm−1 and 887 cm−1 originate from the P = O, P-O-C and P-N bonds respectively, which indicates the successful doping of phosphorus in Arg-CPDs [39,40]. Thus, the surface of Arg-CPDs consists of rich chemical groups (–OH, –COOH, and –NH2). The FT-IR analysis of Arg-CPDs can be supported by XPS spectra. It indicates that the Arg-CPDs is mainly composed of C, N, O, and P elements with atomic ratios of 48.08%, 21.17%, 23.94%, and 6.81%, respectively (Fig. 2b). Specifically, the high-resolution XPS C 1 s spectrum of the Arg-CPDs consist of three peaks that correspond to C-C (284.8 eV), C-O/C-N (286 eV), and CO (288.5 eV) (Fig. 2c). The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum can be resolved into three peaks that correspond to CN-C (399.6 eV), C-N (400.2 eV) and N-H (401.3 eV), respectively (Fig. 2d). The high-resolution O 1 s spectrum can be deconvoluted into four peaks, indicating the presence of P = O (530.4 eV), CO (531.1 eV), P-O (532.3 eV) and C-O (533.2 eV) (Fig. 2e). The high-resolution P 2p spectrum can be fitted as two peaks, indicating the presence of P-O/P = O (133.1 eV) and P-C/P-N (133.9 eV) [39] (Fig. 2f). XPS results further prove that a large number of chemical groups are anchored on the surface of Arg-CPDs, which is consistent with FT-IR results. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy further elucidates the chemical structure of Arg-CPDs to distinguish sp3-hybridized carbon atoms from sp2-hybridized carbon atoms.1H NMR spectrum (Fig. S3a, Supporting Information) of Arg-CPDs shows multiple H signals at 1.3–2.3, 3.2, and 4.2 ppm, representing H atoms on methyl (-CH3) or methylene (-CH2-) groups [40]. In the 13C NMR spectrum (Fig. S3b, Supporting Information) of Arg-CPDs, the signals of 25–60 ppm and 160–180 ppm correspond to C atoms in aliphatic (sp3) and carboxyl/amide groups, respectively [35]. The NMR results further confirm the existence of various chemical groups on the surface of Arg-CPDs, which is consistent with the FT-IR and XPS characterization results. TGA (Fig. S4, Supporting Information) shows that when the temperature rises from 30 °C to 300 °C, the mass loss of Arg-CPDs is 6.1%, which may be caused by the evaporation of water adsorbed on the surface and crystal water. The Arg-CPDs begin to degrade when the temperature exceeds 300 °C, which suggests that Arg-CPDs show favorable thermo-stability. The reason for the thermo-stability may be ascribed to the formation of hydrogen bonds in the Arg-CPDs [34,40]. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) measurements is used to confirm the existence of hydrogen bonds. It (Fig. S5, Supporting Information) shows that there is an obvious endothermic transition at 50–100 °C, stemming from the hydrogen bond breaking within the Arg-CPDs [40]. Accordingly, we propose a possible formation process of Arg-CPDs. Initially, Arg is cross-linked and polymerized under high temperature and phosphoric acid conditions to form oligomers with slight cross-linking. Phosphoric acid is used as an acid catalyst for further dehydration and carbonization as well as a cross-linking agent for polymerization while providing P source for doping. Then, these oligomers are further dehydrated, cross-linked and carbonized to form such CPDs. Therefore, we infer that the surface of Arg-CPDs is surrounded by polymer chains with a carbon skeleton as the core, and a variety of functional groups (–OH, –COOH, and –NH2) are anchored on this intertwined polymer chain. These functional groups can form hydrogen bonds, which can effectively increase the rigidity of molecules. Moreover, these intertwined polymer chains can effectively protect the triplet exciton, and this self-protection has laid the foundation for Arg-CPDs to generate RTP emission. The high-content of the doping N and P elements has also been confirmed, which would facilitate the ISC for effectively populating triplet excitons [40,41].

The UV-Vis absorption and the FL emission and excitation spectra of the Arg-CPDs in aqueous solution are shown in Fig. 3a. The Arg-CPDs exhibits two major absorption peaks around 280 and a broad band from 300 to 400 nm, which may be attributed to the π-π * and n-π * transitions of certain sub-fluorophores containing in the Arg-CPDs, respectively [42,43]. In addition, the peak around 310 nm observed in the UV-Vis absorption spectrum originates from the n-π * transition of CO/CN. Particularly, the presence of CO or CN groups has previously been reported to facilitate the generation of intrinsic triplet excitons through ISC [44,45]. The light yellow Arg-CPDs aqueous solution exhibits strong blue fluorescence under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp. Moreover, the Arg-CPDs aqueous solution exhibits excitation-dependent FL features (Fig. 3b and Fig. S6a, Supporting Information), with emission maxima at 445 nm under the optimal excitation wavelength of 365 nm. Such an excitation-dependent FL feature implies the existence of multiple emissive centers in the Arg-CPDs, which is similar to previous reports [46]. Furthermore, the lifetime (Fig. 3c) and absolute fluorescence quantum yield (QY) (Fig. S6b, Supporting Information) of the Arg-CPDs aqueous solution are 7.59 ns and 13.03%, respectively. To evaluated the fluorescence stability of Arg-CPDs, the possible effects of UV-exposure, high ion intensity, pH and temperature on their fluorescence intensity are considered. As shown in Fig. S7a (Supporting Information), the fluorescence intensity of Arg-CPDs has hardly changed after 120 min continuous irradiation of UV lamp (365 nm). In addition, under the condition of high concentration of NaCl (0–2.5 M) (Fig. S7b, Supporting Information) or pH ranging from 2 to 11 (Fig. S7c, Supporting Information) or temperature increasing from 20 to 60 ℃ (Fig. S7d, Supporting Information), the fluorescence intensity of Arg-CPDs could still maintain more than 90%. However, the fluorescent intensity decreased with the high temperature (60–80 ℃), suggesting the weak tolerance of Arg-CPDs towards the high temperature. These results suggest that the Arg-CPDs could be used as a stable fluorescent ink.

In addition to intense fluorescence Arg-CPDs powder shows an obvious afterglow when the UV lamp is turned off. The digital image ( Fig. 4a) shows that it can still be observed with naked eyes for about 4 s after the UV lamp is turned off (Video S1, Supporting Information). The corresponding fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of Arg-CPDs powder at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm at room temperature (298 K) are shown in Fig. 4b. There is an obvious Stokes shift of about 33 nm. Fig. S8 (Supporting Information) shows the CIE coordinates corresponding to the fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 4b) of Arg-CPDs powder, which is consistent with its warm white fluorescence emission under the UV lamp (Fig. 4a). Moreover, low temperature (77 K) fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra (Fig. S9a, Supporting Information) of the Arg-CPDs are carried out to quantitatively evaluate energy gap (ΔE
ST ) between the lowest triplet (T1) and the lowest singlet (S1). Accordingly, the ΔE
ST value is calculated to be 0.162 eV (S1 =2.431 eV and T1 = 2.269 eV, respectively), and such a small value benefits an effective ISC process used to populate triplet excitons [15,24].

To further explore the RTP origin of Arg-CPDs, the UV/Vis absorption (Fig. 4c) of Arg-CPDs powder is provided and shown two peaks at 280 and 310 nm，respectively, which can be attributed to the π − π* transition of the aromatic CC and n − π* transition of CN/CO groups [42]. These properties are similar to the UV absorption of its aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. 4c，the phosphorescence emission maxima of the Arg-CPDs is observed at 525 nm under the optimal excitation wavelength of 310 nm, which largely overlap with the corresponding absorption peak of 310 nm. Therefore, the n − π* transition of CN or CO groups is considered as one of the phosphorescence and fluorescence origins [4,39]. To further illuminate the RTP properties of Arg-CPDs, time-resolved RTP decay spectrum (Fig. 4d) is measured at 365 nm excitation. According to the ﬁtting formula of the three-exponential function: τ
avg
=
∑
α
i
τ
i
2
/
∑
α
i
τ
i
, the average lifetime is determined as 149 ms (three lifetime components: 8.22 ms (23.4%), 55.56 ms (40.5%), and 345.7 ms (36.1%)), which proves that the persistence emission come from phosphorescence. The multiple phosphorescence lifetimes imply the presence of various electronic transition processes, which originate from the various chemical environments of the CN/CO on the surface of Arg-CPDs. In addition, the phosphorescent lifetime of Arg-CPDs measured at low temperature (77 K) is 667 ms (Fig. S9b, Supporting Information). Therefore, low temperature can effectively inhibit the non-radiative transition caused by molecular vibration and enhance the long-lived triplet excitons for phosphorescence emission [44]. In addition, based on the crosslink-enhanced (CEE) effect [47], we propose a reasonable photoluminescence mechanism of Arg-CPDs. Initially, Arg reacts with phosphoric acid to form the covalently cross-linked frameworks, forming the photoluminescence center and a small energy gap. In addition, the hydrogen bonds formed by intertwined polymer chains restrict the vibration and rotation of their surface groups and increase the rigidity of the whole system. Finally, owing to effective ISC and stabilization of the excited triplet species, the Arg-CPDs powder exhibit strong RTP. Therefore, the reported CEE effect is also deemed to be responsible for the generation of RTP in Arg-CPDs. The FL and RTP emission processes of the Arg-CPDs can be simply illustrated in Fig. 4e.

Inspired by excellent FL and RTP properties of Arg-CPDs, it has demonstrated its potential application as a secure ink for information presentation and encryption. As shown in Fig. 5a, the number “8888″ and the English phrase “STAY WITH US” are written directly on the filter paper by a commercially available pen, which is inhaled into Arg-CPDs ink (40 mg/mL) and Lys-CPDs ink (40 mg/mL) respectively. In FL mode, the paper displays the wrong information with bright white patterns "8888" and "STAY WITH US". The correct information "2468" and "SWU" encrypted by Arg-CPDs ink can only be observed in the phosphorescent mode after the UV is turned off. In addition, the part of the printed “Southwest University” logo coated with Arg-CPDs ink can be clearly identified within a short time after the UV lamp is turned off (Fig. 5b). Consequently, the phosphorescence mode of Arg-CPDs shows higher contrast than the ﬂuorescence mode, suggesting their potential application for the advanced information encryption and anti-counterfeiting.

In summary, we developed a facile, rapid and effective strategy to prepare matrix-free RTP materials through microwave strategy. The obtained Arg-CPDs display remarkably afterglow lifetime of 149 ms and last over 4 s to naked eyes under ambient conditions. TEM, AFM, XRD, FT-IR, XPS and NMR confirm that Arg-CPDs are composed of amorphous carbon cores and intertwined polymer chains. Further investigation reveals that the formed CN/CO bonds promotes the ISC and eﬀectively populates the triple excitons, and a small energy gap of 0.162 eV is formed with CN/CO bonds as the photoluminescence center. In addition, their amorphous polymer-like structure, intraparticle hydrogen bonds and effective doping of N and P elements may play a unique role in the production of RTP. Considering this excellent property, the aqueous solution of Arg-CPDs can be demonstrated as a novel security ink for potential applications such as information protection and encryption. In brief, this work provides an alternative strategy to prepare matrix-free RTP materials, and makes a preliminary exploration of its application.

Chuan Huang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Qinbin Zhang: Investigation, Methodology. Yichuan Zhang: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Mingming Guo: Validation, Resources, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities: Southwest University (5230200472). The authors would like to thank Tianyang Hu from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the phosphorescence analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2023.131456.
